tossups.question: In 1896, Carolus Plummer published a highly influential introduction to a work titled for this author’s Opera Historica. This man engaged in the practice of computus in a “little book on the fleeting and wave-tossed course of time,” his The Reckoning of Time. Roger of Howden wrote a precisely continued sequel to a book by this man, who supposedly died immediately after completing his translation of the (*) Gospel of John. This man was the teacher of Archbishop Egbert. Though he almost never left Wearmouth-Jarrow, the wide usage of this man’s writings in Europe popularized a system invented by Dionysius Exiguus (“eg-ZIG-yoo-uss”) of dating with the currently used BC and AD years. For 10 points, name this Northumbrian monk whose influential chronologies of the Anglo-Saxons include the Ecclesiastical History of the English People.
tossups.question_sanitized: In 1896, Carolus Plummer published a highly influential introduction to a work titled for this author's Opera Historica. This man engaged in the practice of computus in a "little book on the fleeting and wave-tossed course of time," his The Reckoning of Time. Roger of Howden wrote a precisely continued sequel to a book by this man, who supposedly died immediately after completing his translation of the (*) Gospel of John. This man was the teacher of Archbishop Egbert. Though he almost never left Wearmouth-Jarrow, the wide usage of this man's writings in Europe popularized a system invented by Dionysius Exiguus ("eg-ZIG-yoo-uss") of dating with the currently used BC and AD years. For 10 points, name this Northumbrian monk whose influential chronologies of the Anglo-Saxons include the Ecclesiastical History of the English People.

tossups.question: Bertram Colgrave and Robert Mynors edited the Oxford University Press version of one of this man’s works, whose manuscripts are called the "c" and "m" texts. This man took inspiration from Isidore of Seville to write his version of the De natura rerum. One of this man’s works was written in order to calculate the date of (*) Easter and states that the title endeavor can be done using regnal years or 15-year cycles. Besides his On the Reckoning of Time, he wrote a book used extensively by William of Malmsbury and Geoffrey of Monmouth as a source. For 10 points, name this eighth century monk from Jarrow, the author of the Ecclesiastical History of the English People who is called "Venerable."
tossups.answer_sanitized: Saint Bede the Venerable [or the Venerable Bede]
tossups.question_sanitized: Bertram Colgrave and Robert Mynors edited the Oxford University Press version of one of this man's works, whose manuscripts are called the "c" and "m" texts. This man took inspiration from Isidore of Seville to write his version of the De natura rerum. One of this man's works was written in order to calculate the date of (*) Easter and states that the title endeavor can be done using regnal years or 15-year cycles. Besides his On the Reckoning of Time, he wrote a book used extensively by William of Malmsbury and Geoffrey of Monmouth as a source. For 10 points, name this eighth century monk from Jarrow, the author of the Ecclesiastical History of the English People who is called "Venerable."

tossups.question: This man was the first person to correlate the movement of tides to stages of the moon, and he was particularly proud of his calculations in De temporibus, even though one of Bishop Wilfrid’s minions falsely accused him of heresy over the way he calculated the date of Easter. Another controversy over Easter, at the Synod of Whitby, features prominently in his best-known work, a history written for King Ceolfrith of Northumbria in the 730s. For 10 points, name this monk of Jarrow known as the father of English history.
tossups.answer_sanitized: the Venerable Bede [or St. Bede or Baeda]
tossups.question_sanitized: This man was the first person to correlate the movement of tides to stages of the moon, and he was particularly proud of his calculations in De temporibus, even though one of Bishop Wilfrid's minions falsely accused him of heresy over the way he calculated the date of Easter. Another controversy over Easter, at the Synod of Whitby, features prominently in his best-known work, a history written for King Ceolfrith of Northumbria in the 730s. For 10 points, name this monk of Jarrow known as the father of English history.

bonuses.leadin: Much of the earliest surviving literature from the British Isles is in this language or in Latin. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Much of the earliest surviving literature from the British Isles is in this language or in Latin. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this language used by the poets Caedmon (“KAD-mun”) and Cynewulf (“KOO-nay-wolf”), which was also used to write the epic Beowulf.

Caedmon is known for an extant nine-line poem of this type written to “honour / heaven- kingdom’s Ward.” The later authors John Newton and William Cowper (“Cooper”) wrote a collection of these poems named for the town of Olney.
This writer is the namesake of an Old English “Death Song,” which states “before the unavoidable journey,” no one is wiser than he who ponders “good and evil within his soul.”
parts_sanitized: Name this language used by the poets Caedmon ("KAD-mun") and Cynewulf ("KOO-nay-wolf"), which was also used to write the epic Beowulf.

This writer is the namesake of an Old English "Death Song," which states "before the unavoidable journey," no one is wiser than he who ponders "good and evil within his soul."

Bede [or Saint Bede; or the Venerable Bede]

bonuses.leadin_sanitized: A five-line poem attributed to this author states "none is wiser than the man who considers [...] what judgement his soul will receive after its passing". For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author who, according to Cuthbert, reportedly asked for “some pepper, and napkins, and some incense” before composing a poem on their deathbed on Ascension Day, 735.
Bede’s death song is the poem in this language that survives in the largest number of manuscripts. The Exeter Book contains many poems in this language used to write Beowulf.

The Exeter Book contains this 143-line poem which surveys the Heroic Age of Northern Europe. This poem gives a list of tribes and writes of how “Hrothwulf and Hrothgar held longest the kinship together”.

bonuses.leadin: This author identified the Dioscuri with the Alci, whom he claimed that a priest of the Nahanarvali worshiped while dressed as a woman. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author who claimed that peoples like the Suebi sacrificed animals to Mars and Hercules in his ethnographic text Germania, which traces their descent from the divine Tuisto.

Following Tacitus, this Latin noun precedes “romana” or “graeca” (“GRY-kah”) to describe the identification of foreign gods with putative Greco-Roman equivalents. This noun precedes “germanica” in a converse translation of weekday names.

This historian recorded belief in interpretatio christiana, the inculturation of pagan practices by missionaries. This “Venerable” English historian explained Easter customs with the Germanic goddess Ēostre.
answers: interpretatio [or interpretatione; accept interpretatio romana or interpretatione romana; accept interpretatio graeca or interpretatione graeca; accept interpretatio germanica or interpretatione germanica]
Bede [or the Venerable Bede, Bede the Venerable, Saint Bede, Bǣda, Bēda, or Beda Venerabilis]

bonuses.leadin: When Gregory the Great learned that slaves from this kingdom were “Angles,” he punned “more like angels, if they were Christian.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this kingdom that Gregory said would be rescued “from wrath” based on its name and should “sing Alleluia” because its king was Ælla in two more bad Latin puns. Oswiu tried to seize this kingdom from Oswine.
Gregory the Great’s puns are related in this author’s Ecclesiastical History of the English People, which he wrote at a monastery in Jarrow in Northumbria.

Gregory the Great sent a missionary of this name as his “Apostle to the English.” Another saint with this name exchanged extensive correspondence with his student Quodvultdeus (“KWOAD-wult-DAY-oos”), whose name means “what God wills.”
answers: Deira [or Kingdom of Deira; or Deifr; accept Oswine of Deira; prompt on Northumbria; reject “Northumberland”] (Gregory thought Deira would be rescued de ira, or “from wrath.”)
Bede [or Saint Bede; or the Venerable Bede; or Bede the Venerable]
Augustine [accept Saint Augustine of Hippo or Aurelius Augustinus or Aurelius Augustinus Hipponensis; accept Saint Augustine of Canterbury]

packet.name: Prelims 3. Florida A, Western A, Imperial A

bonuses.leadin: One of the four surviving manuscripts of poetry in this language ended up, for unknown reasons, in the Italian town of Vercelli ("ver-CHELL-ee"). For 10 points each:
parts: Name this language whose other poetic manuscripts include the Exeter Book. Beowulf was written in this language.

Perhaps the best-known Old English poetic manuscript is this codex, which contains the only copy of Beowulf as well as a translation of Judith. The edges of its pages were burned in the 1731 Ashburnham House fire, from which most of it was narrowly saved.
The oldest Old English poem, Caedmon's ("KIDE-mon's") Hymn, survives only in copies of this medieval scholar's work Ecclesiastical History of the English People.
answers_sanitized: Old English [or Anglo-Saxon; prompt on English]
Nowell Codex [or Cotton MS Vitellius A XV]
The Venerable Bede [or Saint Bede or Bæda]

bonuses.leadin: This author praised "the man who considers...what good or evil he has done, and what judgement his soul will receive after its passing" in his five-line "Death Song." For 10 points each:
parts: Name this mentor of Alcuin of York. He preserved the legend of Caedmon and his namesake hymn in a history written in Jarrow.

Caedmon's Hymn, possibly the oldest surviving English poem, was one of the oldest poems to use this poetic device. This repetitive device, also used in the Poetic Edda, typically replaced rhyme in Old English poetry.

Along with saints' lives, these works make up the majority of surviving Old English prose. Examples of these works include one that Wulfstan punningly titled "Lupi ad Anglos," as well as Jonathan Edwards's much later "Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God."
answers_sanitized: Venerable Bede [or Saint Bede the Venerable]
sermons [or homilies; or Sermo Lupi ad Anglos]

In Bede’s (“beedʼs”) account, Caedmon (“CAD-mun”) gained the ability to sing during one of these events. One of these events “of the Rood” titles a poem, attributed to Caedmon, in which the cross narrates Jesus’s crucifixion.

Bede wrote texts of this type in the Aenigmata section of his Liber epigrammatum. “An onion” is the answer to a suggestive text of this kind in the Exeter Book, which likely inspired a contest of these things in The Hobbit.

Before canonization, an individual undergoes this process to be called "Blessed." To be considered for this process, a deceased individual must have performed at least one posthumous (POSS-chuh-miss) miracle.

bonuses.leadin: The stories of Leir and Cymbeline were adapted by Shakespeare from this man's Historia Regum Brittaniae, or History of the Kings of Britain. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this historian who began that text with the settling of Britain by Brutus, a great grandson of Aeneas.

bonuses.leadin: For 10 points each, identify the following about calculating time during the pre-modern era.
parts: This device, inspired by the ancients, consisted of four quadrants that had rotating parts which allowed one to locate the position of the stars and other celestial bodies. It allowed one to determine what time of day it was.

An exact way of calculating the change in the amount of daylight that existed per a given day was pioneered in this author’s The Reckoning of Time. This monk is best known for his An Ecclesiastical History of the English Peoples.
In 1582, a pope of this name reformed the then popular Julian calendar to account for eleven missing minutes at the end of each year.
